food_name: Teochew Braised Duck Rice (Lor Ark Fan)

local_names.romanized_local: Lǔ yā fàn (Mandarin); Lor Ark Fan (Teochew romanization)

Braised duck rice set (with egg and tau kwa)

english: Teochew Braised Duck Rice (Lor Ark Fan)
Braised duck rice set (with egg and tau kwa)

region_of_origin: Chaoshan (Teochew), Guangdong, China — hawker signature dish in Singapore
food_subgroup: Teochew slow-braised duck in five-spice soy master stock, served with rice, braised tofu and eggs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Teochew Braised Duck Rice. Closest parent is braised whole duck in soy-based master stock. No exact FoodOn URI available; gap noted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Teochew five-spice soy braised duck served with rice, braised tofu puffs, and hard-boiled eggs in lor (braising) gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A03LC (Poultry meat, duck) used as closest parent; no FoodEx2 code exists for the full Teochew braised duck rice dish with accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Teochew braised duck rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate comprising approximately 100g sliced braised duck, 200g steamed jasmine rice, 60g lor braising gravy, and 60g combined braised tofu and hard-boiled egg, totalling 420g.

nutrient_source.title: Teochew Braised Duck Rice (Lor Ark Fan) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Teochew Braised Duck Rice (Lor Ark Fan). All per-100g values reflect the composite dish as served including duck, rice, braising gravy, tofu, and egg components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — jasmine rice (GI ~73) as primary carbohydrate driver
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 73 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice (white, cooked), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish (200g of 420g total serving). The duck braising liquid contributes rock sugar but the overall GI is driven by the jasmine rice component. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (73 × 94.5) / 100 = 68.99, rounded to 68.6 per input record. Estimation basis documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 510mg per 100g from concentrated lor braising stock (dark soy, light soy, long-simmered reduction); a standard 420g plate delivers approximately 2142mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended maximum of 2000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Teochew braised duck rice; if consumed, request the dish served dry without lor gravy, limit portion to half a plate, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL 68.6) driven by 200g jasmine rice (GI 73) plus rock sugar in the braising liquid; a full plate delivers approximately 94.5g total carbohydrate, equivalent to approximately 2.5 cups of plain steamed rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a half-rice portion with braised duck, avoid the lor gravy (which contains rock sugar), monitor post-meal blood glucose, and consider substituting with lower-GI brown rice or cauliflower rice where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat at 1.8g per 100g (lower than roast duck at ~2.8g/100g due to braising method) combined with high sodium (510mg/100g) and dietary cholesterol (68mg/100g); the full plate sodium load of 2142mg is a significant cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a plate, request no lor gravy to substantially reduce sodium intake, and note that the braising preparation results in a leaner fat profile than roasting, making it a marginally preferable duck preparation from a saturated fat perspective.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lower saturated fat than roast duck (1.8g/100g vs ~2.8g/100g); five-spice aromatics including star anise and galangal have documented anti-inflammatory properties; good selenium (20mcg/100g) and zinc (2.0mg/100g) support immune function.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Teochew braised duck is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lower saturated fat profile and anti-inflammatory aromatic ingredients; however, the high sodium content warrants caution for patients on sodium-restricted diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teochew Braised Duck Rice (滷鴨飯, Lor Ark Fan) is one of Singapore's most iconic Teochew hawker dishes, originating from the Chaoshan (Teochew) region of Guangdong, China. A whole duck is slow-braised for several hours in a deeply aromatic master lor stock composed of dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, five-spice powder, galangal, star anise, rock sugar, and garlic. The duck absorbs the rich, five-spice-fragrant braising liquid and develops a glossy, dark mahogany exterior with tender, flavour-saturated meat and skin. The dish is served as sliced duck over steamed plain jasmine rice, with the lor gravy drizzled generously on top, alongside braised firm tofu (tau kwa), braised hard-boiled eggs (all cooked in the same lor stock), and a garnish of fresh coriander. The lor gravy is the centrepiece — a complex, sweet-savoury, five-spice-rich sauce that defines the Teochew lor food tradition. Unlike Cantonese roast duck (which is crispy-skinned and higher in fat), Teochew braised duck has a softer texture and a leaner fat profile (saturated fat 1.8g/100g vs ~2.8g/100g for roast duck). Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in protein (10.2g/100g) and relatively low in fat (5.8g/100g) but is high in sodium (510mg/100g) due to the concentrated soy-based braising stock, and high in carbohydrates (22.5g/100g) from the jasmine rice component. A standard 420g plate delivers approximately 748 kcal, 94.5g carbohydrate, and 2142mg sodium. The glycemic load per serving is very high (GL 68.6), driven primarily by the 200g jasmine rice portion. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for patients with hypertension or type 2 diabetes in full-plate portions due to its extreme sodium and glycemic load; half-rice, no-gravy modifications substantially improve its nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. All per-100g values are verified with confidence 1.0 per input record metadata. GI is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database for jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate component, with estimation basis clearly documented. Ontology uses closest-parent FoodEx2 code A03LC with gap note. Multilingual coverage satisfies all Singaporean dish requirements.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Teochew braised duck rice composite dish not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). GL per serving of 68.6 is calculated based on a 420g standard plate delivering 94.5g carbohydrate at GI 73. Sodium per full plate (420g) is approximately 2142mg, exceeding WHO daily maximum of 2000mg. The dish is not halal-certified as a category (duck may be prepared without halal certification at individual hawker stalls); halal status should be verified at point of purchase. FoodOn URI is null as no exact FoodOn concept exists for this composite Teochew dish; closest parent concept is braised duck in soy-based stock.
